seoTitle: Recom Technical Support | DC-DC Converter Selection & Design Guides
seoDescription: Access Recom technical documentation, selection guides, and application notes. Expert FAE support for power module design, thermal management, and system integration from authorized distributor.

faqs.question: Where can I find Recom product documentation and datasheets?
faqs.answer: Recom product documentation is available through multiple channels: BeiLuo Electronics website - comprehensive product pages with datasheets, application notes, and reference designs; Recom website - complete technical documentation library; Direct contact - our FAE team can provide specific documents and answer technical questions. All documentation includes detailed specifications, electrical characteristics, thermal data, and application guidance. Contact us for the latest documentation or specific technical resources.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team for documentation requests or specific technical resources.

faqs.question: How do I get technical support for my Recom design?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo Electronics provides comprehensive technical support for Recom designs: Application engineering - schematic review, PCB layout guidance, thermal analysis; Product selection - helping choose optimal modules for your requirements; Design validation - prototype testing and troubleshooting; Production support - manufacturing guidance and quality assurance. Support channels: Direct FAE contact for complex designs; Email support for documentation and general questions; Phone support for urgent issues. Our FAE team has extensive Recom experience and can guide you through the entire design process.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your design requirements and support needs.

faqs.question: What design tools are available for Recom products?
faqs.answer: Recom provides several design tools to assist with power system development: Online product selectors - filter by specifications to find the right module; Thermal calculators - for heatsink selection and thermal analysis; Efficiency calculators - for power loss and efficiency predictions; Application notes - detailed design guidance for specific applications. Access: Most tools available on Recom website; BeiLuo FAE team can assist with tool usage and interpretation of results. Contact us for tool access and training.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for design tool access and assistance with power system design.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Recom module for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Recom module involves several key steps: First, define your electrical requirements - input voltage range, output voltage, output current or power, and isolation needs. Second, consider efficiency requirements - Recom offers modules from 80% to 90% efficiency. Third, evaluate thermal constraints - calculate power dissipation and ensure adequate cooling. Fourth, determine package requirements - SIP, DIP, or SMD based on board space and assembly. Fifth, consider certification needs - medical, automotive, or industrial standards. Our FAE team can guide you through the selection process.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for module selection guidance and application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the process for getting Recom samples?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo Electronics provides Recom samples for evaluation and prototyping: Sample request process: Contact our sales or FAE team with your requirements; Provide project information and expected production volumes; Samples typically ship within 1-3 business days for stock items. Evaluation support: We provide evaluation boards for many Recom modules; Technical documentation and application notes included; FAE support available for evaluation assistance. For production planning, we recommend requesting samples early in the design cycle.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team to request samples and discuss your evaluation requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate thermal requirements for Recom modules?
faqs.answer: Thermal design for Recom modules involves these steps: Calculate power dissipation using P_loss = P_out × (1/η - 1), where η is efficiency at your operating point. Determine maximum allowable case temperature from datasheet (typically +105°C). Calculate required thermal resistance: Rth = (T_case_max - T_ambient) / P_loss. Select heatsink or ensure adequate copper area for thermal management. Recom SIP/DIP packages typically don't require heatsinks for rated power at normal ambient temperatures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for thermal design assistance and heatsink selection guidance.

faqs.question: What training resources are available for Recom products?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo Electronics offers various training resources for Recom products: Technical seminars - periodic training sessions on Recom products and power design; Application notes - detailed guides for specific applications and design techniques; Online resources - technical articles and design guides; FAE consultations - one-on-one training for your specific application. Topics covered include: Product selection and sizing; Thermal design and management; PCB layout best practices; EMI/EMC design; Medical safety requirements. Contact us to schedule training.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to schedule training or request specific technical education resources.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot Recom module issues?
faqs.answer: Troubleshooting Recom modules involves systematic diagnosis: Check input voltage - ensure within specified range; Verify output loading - ensure within rated capacity; Measure temperatures - check for overheating; Inspect connections - verify proper mounting and electrical connections. Common issues and solutions: No output - check input voltage and polarity; Overheating - verify thermal design and airflow; Output regulation issues - check load conditions and input stability. Our FAE team can assist with troubleshooting complex issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for troubleshooting assistance and root cause analysis.

articles.title: How to Select Recom DC-DC Converters for Your Application
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting Recom DC-DC converters based on input/output requirements, isolation needs, package preferences, and application constraints.
articles.content:
## Introduction to DC-DC Converter Selection

Selecting the right DC-DC converter is critical for the success of your power system design. This guide provides a systematic approach to choosing Recom DC-DC converters based on your specific application requirements.

## Step 1: Define Input Requirements

### Input Voltage Range
The first step is to determine your input voltage characteristics:

- **Nominal voltage**: What is your typical operating voltage?
- **Voltage variation**: How much does the input vary?
- **Transient conditions**: Are there voltage spikes or sags?

Recom offers two main input range options:
- **2:1 range**: Covers 2:1 ratio (e.g., 9-18V for 12V nominal)
- **4:1 range**: Covers 4:1 ratio (e.g., 9-36V for 12V/24V systems)

### Input Source Characteristics
Consider your power source:
- **Battery systems**: Use 4:1 range for battery voltage variation
- **Regulated DC bus**: 2:1 range offers better efficiency
- **Automotive**: 4:1 range handles load dump and cold crank

## Step 2: Determine Output Requirements

### Voltage and Current
Calculate your load requirements:
- Output voltage needed
- Maximum output current
- Total output power (P = V × I)

Add 20-30% margin for reliability and future expansion.

### Regulation Requirements
Consider load regulation needs:
- **Tight regulation**: Choose regulated converters
- **Loose regulation**: Unregulated may be acceptable
- **Line regulation**: Important for varying input conditions

## Step 3: Isolation Requirements

### When Isolation is Required
Galvanic isolation is necessary for:
- Safety-critical applications
- Breaking ground loops
- Level shifting between voltage domains
- Medical equipment (patient safety)
- Industrial noise immunity

### Isolation Voltage Levels
Recom offers various isolation levels:
- **1000VDC**: Basic industrial applications
- **1600VDC**: Standard industrial grade
- **3000VDC**: High isolation applications
- **6000VDC**: Maximum isolation for harsh environments

## Step 4: Package Selection

### SIP (Single In-line Package)
- Compact vertical design
- 7-pin or 8-pin configuration
- Ideal for space-constrained designs
- Through-hole mounting

### DIP (Dual In-line Package)
- Standard horizontal layout
- 14-pin to 24-pin options
- Better thermal performance
- Easy prototyping and assembly

### SMD (Surface Mount Device)
- Automated assembly compatible
- Smallest footprint
- Requires reflow soldering
- Best for high-volume production

## Step 5: Environmental Considerations

### Temperature Range
Recom converters operate from -40°C to +85°C without derating. Consider:
- Ambient temperature extremes
- Self-heating from power dissipation
- Thermal management requirements

### Mechanical Stress
For harsh environments, consider:
- Vibration resistance
- Shock resistance
- Humidity and moisture protection

## Step 6: Certification Requirements

### Safety Certifications
Ensure your application requirements are met:
- **UL/EN/IEC 62368-1**: Information technology equipment
- **IEC 60601-1**: Medical equipment (2xMOPP)
- **AEC-Q100**: Automotive applications

### EMC Compliance
Consider electromagnetic compatibility:
- Conducted emissions
- Radiated emissions
- Immunity requirements

## Application Examples

### Industrial Automation
For PLC power systems:
- Input: 24VDC nominal (18-36V range)
- Output: 5V at 1A, isolated
- Package: SIP for compact design
- Temperature: -40°C to +85°C
- Recommended: R05P05S

### Medical Equipment
For patient-connected devices:
- Input: 12VDC from medical-grade supply
- Output: 5V isolated with 2xMOPP
- Leakage: <100μA for CF rating
- Recommended: REM3.5-1205S

### Battery-Powered Systems
For 12V battery applications:
- Input: 9-18V (2:1 range)
- Output: 3.3V at 500mA
- Efficiency: Maximize battery life
- Recommended: R-78E3.3-0.5

## Design Checklist

Before finalizing your selection:

- [ ] Input voltage range covers all operating conditions
- [ ] Output power has 20-30% margin
- [ ] Isolation level meets safety requirements
- [ ] Package fits mechanical constraints
- [ ] Temperature range covers environment
- [ ] Required certifications are met
- [ ] Efficiency meets system requirements
- [ ] External component count is acceptable

## Conclusion

Proper DC-DC converter selection requires careful consideration of electrical, mechanical, and environmental requirements. Recom's comprehensive product portfolio offers solutions for virtually any application. Contact our FAE team for assistance with your specific design requirements.

relatedArticles.title: Thermal Design Guide for Recom Power Modules
EMC Design Guide for Recom Converters

faeInsights.insight: The most common mistake in DC-DC converter selection is not accounting for worst-case conditions. Always design for maximum load at minimum input voltage and highest ambient temperature. The 4:1 input range converters are more versatile but slightly less efficient than 2:1 versions - choose based on your actual input variation needs.
faeInsights.logic: Selection process: First, characterize your input power source including all variations and transients. Second, calculate worst-case load requirements with margin. Third, determine if isolation is needed for safety or noise immunity. Fourth, select package based on space and assembly constraints. Fifth, verify environmental ratings meet your application.
keyTakeaways: Always design for worst-case conditions
4:1 range for battery/unstable sources, 2:1 for stable DC bus
Isolation required for safety and ground loop elimination
Package selection affects thermal performance and assembly
Include 20-30% power margin for reliability
commonPitfalls: Not accounting for input voltage transients
Insufficient power margin for peak loads
Selecting non-medical grade for patient applications
bestPractices: Characterize input source under all conditions
Measure actual load currents with margin
Use 4:1 range for battery applications
Verify thermal design with actual conditions
Consult FAE for medical/safety-critical applications
troubleshootingTips: If converter won't start, check minimum input voltage
Output ripple can be reduced with additional capacitance

customerCases.challenge: Needed isolated 5V from 24V industrial bus with wide voltage variations
customerCases.solution: Selected R05P05S with 4:1 input range for 9-36V operation
customerCases.result: Reliable operation across all factory conditions, passed EMC testing

faqs.question: What is the difference between regulated and unregulated converters?
faqs.answer: Regulated converters maintain constant output voltage despite input voltage variations and load changes. Unregulated converters have output that varies with input voltage (typically ±5-10%). Use regulated converters when precise output voltage is required, unregulated when input is stable and cost is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose regulated for precise voltage requirements, unregulated for cost-sensitive stable input applications.

faqs.question: How much input voltage margin do I need?
faqs.answer: For reliable operation: Minimum input should be at least 10% above converter's minimum rated voltage; Maximum input should be at least 10% below converter's maximum rated voltage. This accounts for transients, measurement tolerances, and aging. For battery applications, account for end-of-life voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Include 10% margin on both minimum and maximum input voltages for reliable operation.

articles.title: Thermal Design Guide for Recom Power Modules
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for thermal design of power systems using Recom modules, including thermal calculations, PCB layout, and cooling considerations.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Thermal Design

Proper thermal design is essential for reliable power supply operation. This guide covers thermal management for Recom DC-DC converters and AC-DC power supplies.

## Understanding Power Dissipation

### Calculating Power Loss
Power dissipation in a DC-DC converter:

```
P_loss = P_out × (1/η - 1)
```

Where:
- P_out = Output power (W)
- η = Efficiency (decimal)

Example: For 5W output at 85% efficiency:
```
P_loss = 5 × (1/0.85 - 1) = 5 × 0.176 = 0.88W
```

### Efficiency Variations
Note that efficiency varies with:
- Input voltage (typically lower at extremes)
- Output load (usually specified at 100% load)
- Temperature (may decrease at high temperatures)

Always use worst-case efficiency for thermal design.

## Thermal Resistance Concepts

### Thermal Resistance Model
Heat flows from junction to ambient through thermal resistances:

```
T_junction = T_ambient + P_loss × (R_thJC + R_thCH + R_thHA)
```

Where:
- R_thJC = Junction to case thermal resistance
- R_thCH = Case to heatsink thermal resistance
- R_thHA = Heatsink to ambient thermal resistance

### Recom Converter Thermal Characteristics
Recom SIP/DIP package thermal resistances:
- R_thJC (junction to case): ~15-25°C/W
- R_thCA (case to ambient): ~40-60°C/W (without heatsink)

## PCB Thermal Design

### Copper Area for Heat Spreading
For converters without heatsinks, PCB copper area provides cooling:

| Copper Area | Thermal Resistance |
|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 cm² | ~80°C/W |
| 5 cm² | ~50°C/W |
| 10 cm² | ~40°C/W |
| 20 cm² | ~35°C/W |

### PCB Layout Guidelines

1. **Thermal Vias**: Use thermal vias under the converter
   - 1.0-1.2mm diameter
   - 0.3-0.5mm drill
   - Copper filled for best conductivity

2. **Copper Planes**: Connect to internal copper planes
   - Use multiple vias to spread heat
   - Connect to ground and power planes

3. **Component Placement**: Allow airflow around converter
   - Keep clearance for natural convection
   - Avoid placing heat-sensitive components nearby

## Heatsink Selection

### When to Use Heatsinks
Heatsinks are typically not required for Recom converters at rated power up to 60°C ambient. Consider heatsinks when:
- Ambient temperature exceeds 60°C
- Power dissipation exceeds 1W
- Enclosure has limited airflow
- Reliability is critical

### Heatsink Selection Process

1. **Calculate Required Thermal Resistance**:
```
R_th_required = (T_case_max - T_ambient) / P_loss
```

2. **Select Heatsink**: Choose heatsink with R_th less than required

3. **Include Interface Resistance**: Add 0.5-1.0°C/W for thermal interface

### Example Calculation

Given:
- Output power: 10W
- Efficiency: 86%
- Ambient temperature: 50°C
- Maximum case temperature: 105°C

Calculations:
```
P_loss = 10 × (1/0.86 - 1) = 1.63W
R_th_required = (105 - 50) / 1.63 = 33.7°C/W
```

Without heatsink (R_thCA = 45°C/W):
```
T_case = 50 + 1.63 × 45 = 123°C (TOO HOT!)
```

With heatsink (R_thHA = 15°C/W + 1°C/W interface):
```
T_case = 50 + 1.63 × 16 = 76°C (OK)
```

## Thermal Interface Materials

### Types of Interface Materials

1. **Thermal Grease**:
   - Best thermal performance (0.1-0.5°C-cm²/W)
   - Messy application
   - Can pump out over time

2. **Thermal Pads**:
   - Easy application
   - Moderate performance (0.5-2.0°C-cm²/W)
   - Good for gap filling

3. **Phase Change Materials**:
   - Good performance (0.3-0.8°C-cm²/W)
   - Easy application
   - Long-term stability

### Application Guidelines

- Apply thin, even layer
- Ensure full contact area coverage
- Minimize interface thickness
- Use mounting pressure per manufacturer spec

## Enclosure and Airflow

### Natural Convection
For natural convection cooling:
- Provide ventilation openings
- Orient enclosure for chimney effect
- Keep converter away from walls
- Use thermally conductive enclosure materials

### Forced Air Cooling
For high-power or high-temperature applications:
- Use fans for airflow of 100-200 LFM
- Position intake away from exhaust
- Filter air to prevent dust buildup
- Consider fan reliability and noise

### Sealed Enclosures
For sealed or IP-rated enclosures:
- Use external heatsinks with thermal vias
- Consider heat pipes for heat transfer
- Design for worst-case ambient temperature
- May require derating

## Thermal Testing and Validation

### Temperature Measurement

1. **Thermocouples**: Attach to case with thermal epoxy
2. **Infrared Thermometer**: Measure case temperature
3. **Thermal Camera**: Visualize hot spots

### Testing Procedure

1. Measure ambient temperature
2. Apply maximum load
3. Allow 30 minutes for thermal stabilization
4. Measure case temperature
5. Verify within rated limits

### Margin Requirements

Design with margin for reliable operation:
- Keep case temperature 10-20°C below maximum
- Account for altitude (derate 1°C per 1000ft)
- Consider component aging effects

## Special Considerations

### High Altitude
At high altitude (>3000ft):
- Reduced air density decreases cooling
- Derate maximum ambient by 1°C per 1000ft
- Consider forced air cooling

### High Humidity
In high humidity environments:
- Ensure adequate spacing for creepage
- Use conformal coating if required
- Consider condensation effects

### Multi-Module Systems
For systems with multiple converters:
- Spread modules across PCB
- Avoid thermal shadowing
- Calculate total power dissipation
- Ensure adequate overall cooling

## Design Examples

### Example 1: PLC Power Supply
- Converter: R12P21503D (15W dual output)
- Ambient: 55°C in sealed enclosure
- Solution: Added 20cm² copper area, positioned near enclosure wall
- Result: Case temperature 78°C (within limits)

### Example 2: Medical Device
- Converter: REM3.5-1205S (3.5W medical)
- Ambient: 40°C with natural convection
- Solution: Standard PCB layout with thermal vias
- Result: Case temperature 52°C (well within limits)

## Conclusion

Proper thermal design ensures reliable operation and long converter lifetime. Most Recom converters at rated power require only adequate PCB copper area for cooling. For high temperatures or high power, additional thermal management may be required. Contact our FAE team for assistance with challenging thermal designs.

relatedArticles.title: How to Select Recom DC-DC Converters
EMC Design Guide for Recom Converters

faeInsights.insight: Most thermal issues with Recom converters are caused by insufficient PCB copper area or poor airflow in enclosures. The SIP and DIP packages are designed to operate at full power without heatsinks up to 60°C ambient when properly mounted on PCB with adequate copper. For high-temperature applications, focus on maximizing copper area and ensuring airflow rather than adding heatsinks.
faeInsights.logic: Thermal design process: First, calculate power dissipation using worst-case efficiency. Second, determine maximum allowable case temperature from datasheet. Third, calculate required thermal resistance to ambient. Fourth, design PCB with adequate copper area. Fifth, verify with thermal testing under actual conditions.
keyTakeaways: Calculate power dissipation using worst-case efficiency
PCB copper area is primary cooling method
Most converters don't need heatsinks up to 60°C
Test under actual operating conditions
Include margin for altitude and aging
commonPitfalls: Insufficient PCB copper area for heat spreading
Not accounting for enclosure temperature rise
bestPractices: Use generous copper area for heat spreading
Implement thermal vias to inner planes
Allow clearance for natural convection
Test at maximum ambient temperature
Include 20% thermal margin in design
troubleshootingTips: If overheating, increase copper area first
Check for blocked airflow in enclosure

customerCases.challenge: Converter overheating in 70°C ambient sealed enclosure
customerCases.solution: Increased PCB copper area to 25cm² and added thermal vias to inner ground plane
customerCases.result: Case temperature reduced from 115°C to 82°C, within specification

faqs.question: Do I need a heatsink for my Recom converter?
faqs.answer: Most Recom converters at rated power do not require heatsinks when mounted on PCB with adequate copper area (typically 10-20cm²) at ambient temperatures up to 60°C. Heatsinks are only needed for: high ambient temperatures (>60°C), high power dissipation (>1.5W), or sealed enclosures with no airflow. Calculate your specific thermal requirements using the methods in this guide.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate thermal requirements using the formulas in this guide. Contact FAE if case temperature exceeds 90°C.

faqs.question: How much copper area do I need?
faqs.answer: As a general guideline: For <1W dissipation: 5-10cm²; For 1-2W dissipation: 10-20cm²; For >2W dissipation: 20cm² or more. Use thermal vias to connect to internal planes for better heat spreading. These are guidelines - always calculate your specific requirements and test under actual conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 15-20cm² for typical applications, calculate specific requirements, and verify with testing.

articles.title: EMC Design Guide for Recom Converters
articles.summary: Guide for achieving EMC compliance in systems using Recom converters, including EMI filtering, layout practices, and troubleshooting.
articles.content:
## Introduction to EMC Design

Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) is essential for reliable operation of power systems. This guide covers EMC design practices for Recom DC-DC converters.

## Understanding EMC Requirements

### Emissions vs Immunity
EMC involves two aspects:
- **Emissions**: Minimizing electromagnetic interference generated by your system
- **Immunity**: Ensuring your system operates correctly in the presence of external interference

### Standards Overview
Common EMC standards for power supplies:
- **EN 55032/CISPR 32**: Multimedia equipment emissions
- **EN 55011/CISPR 11**: Industrial equipment emissions
- **IEC 61000-4-x**: Immunity standards

## Input Filtering

### Conducted Emissions
Input filtering reduces conducted emissions on power lines:

1. **Differential Mode Filtering**:
   - Use X-capacitors across input lines
   - Add differential mode inductors
   - Typical values: 0.1-1μF X-cap, 1-10mH inductor

2. **Common Mode Filtering**:
   - Use Y-capacitors to ground
   - Add common mode chokes
   - Typical values: 1000-4700pF Y-cap, 1-10mH CM choke

### Filter Design Guidelines

- Place filter close to converter input
- Minimize trace lengths between filter and converter
- Use shielded inductors for high-frequency attenuation
- Consider resonant frequencies of filter components

## Output Filtering

### Output Ripple Reduction
Reduce output ripple and noise:

1. **Output Capacitors**:
   - Use ceramic capacitors for high-frequency noise
   - Add electrolytic capacitors for low-frequency ripple
   - Place capacitors close to load

2. **Post-Filtering**:
   - Add LC filter for sensitive loads
   - Use ferrite beads for high-frequency attenuation
   - Consider active filtering for precision applications

## PCB Layout for EMC

### Grounding Strategy

1. **Minimize Ground Loops**:
   - Use star grounding for sensitive circuits
   - Separate power and signal grounds
   - Connect grounds at single point

2. **Ground Plane Usage**:
   - Use continuous ground plane when possible
   - Avoid slots or cuts in ground plane
   - Provide multiple vias for ground connections

### Trace Routing

1. **High-Frequency Loops**:
   - Minimize loop area of high-frequency currents
   - Keep input and output traces close together
   - Use twisted pairs for external connections

2. **Sensitive Signals**:
   - Route sensitive signals away from power traces
   - Use guard rings for high-impedance circuits
   - Implement proper shielding when needed

## Shielding and Enclosure

### When to Use Shielding

Consider shielding when:
- Emissions exceed limits without shielding
- System operates in high-RF environment
- Sensitive analog circuits need protection
- Regulatory requirements mandate shielding

### Shielding Best Practices

1. **Enclosure Design**:
   - Use conductive materials (metal, conductive plastic)
   - Ensure continuous contact at seams
   - Provide adequate ventilation openings

2. **Cable Shielding**:
   - Use shielded cables for I/O connections
   - Properly terminate cable shields
   - Minimize unshielded cable lengths

## Troubleshooting EMC Issues

### Common Problems and Solutions

1. **High Conducted Emissions**:
   - Increase input filter attenuation
   - Check filter component values
   - Verify filter grounding

2. **High Radiated Emissions**:
   - Improve PCB layout
   - Add shielding
   - Reduce loop areas

3. **Immunity Failures**:
   - Add transient protection
   - Improve grounding
   - Increase filtering

### Measurement Techniques

1. **Near-Field Probing**:
   - Use near-field probes to locate emission sources
   - Identify high-frequency current paths
   - Evaluate shielding effectiveness

2. **Current Probing**:
   - Measure common mode and differential mode currents
   - Verify filter performance
   - Identify coupling paths

## Design Checklist

Before EMC testing:

- [ ] Input filter designed and implemented
- [ ] Output filtering adequate for load requirements
- [ ] PCB layout follows EMC best practices
- [ ] Grounding strategy properly implemented
- [ ] Shielding designed if required
- [ ] Cables properly shielded and routed
- [ ] Pre-compliance testing completed

## Conclusion

Good EMC design requires attention to filtering, layout, and shielding. Recom converters are designed with EMC considerations, but system-level design is critical for meeting regulatory requirements. Contact our FAE team for assistance with challenging EMC designs.

relatedArticles.title: How to Select Recom DC-DC Converters
Thermal Design Guide for Recom Power Modules

faeInsights.insight: Most EMC issues with DC-DC converters can be traced to inadequate input filtering or poor PCB layout. The switching frequency and harmonics are the primary emission sources. A properly designed input filter with both common mode and differential mode components is essential for meeting conducted emissions limits.
faeInsights.logic: EMC design process: First, understand your emission requirements and standards. Second, design input filter based on converter switching characteristics. Third, implement proper PCB layout with attention to high-frequency loops. Fourth, add output filtering if load is sensitive. Fifth, test early and iterate as needed.
keyTakeaways: Input filtering is critical for conducted emissions
PCB layout has major impact on radiated emissions
Minimize high-frequency current loop areas
Test early in design process
Shielding is last resort after layout optimization
commonPitfalls: Poor PCB layout with large loop areas
bestPractices: Design filter based on switching frequency
Place filter components close to converter
Use continuous ground plane
Minimize trace lengths in high-frequency paths
Test with representative cables and loads
troubleshootingTips: Use near-field probes to locate emission sources
Check filter component values and placement
Verify ground connections are low impedance

customerCases.challenge: Failed conducted emissions testing at 150kHz-1MHz range
customerCases.solution: Added common mode choke and increased X-capacitor value
customerCases.result: Emissions reduced by 15dB, passed Class B with 6dB margin

faqs.question: What filter values should I use for Recom converters?
faqs.answer: Typical filter values for Recom DC-DC converters: X-capacitor 0.22-0.47μF; Y-capacitor 1000-2200pF; Common mode choke 1-4.7mH. Exact values depend on switching frequency and required attenuation. For 2W converters, start with 0.22μF X-cap and 2.2mH CM choke.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for filter design assistance based on your specific converter and requirements.

faqs.question: How do I measure conducted emissions?
faqs.answer: Conducted emissions are measured using Line Impedance Stabilization Networks (LISN) and spectrum analyzer: Connect LISN between power source and EUT; Measure voltage on both line and neutral; Scan frequency range (typically 150kHz-30MHz); Compare to Class A or Class B limits. Pre-compliance testing can be done with lower-cost equipment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for guidance on EMC testing and pre-compliance measurement.

articles.title: Medical Power Supply Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for designing medical power supplies using Recom medical-grade converters, covering safety standards, isolation, and leakage current.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Medical Power Design

Medical power supplies must meet stringent safety requirements to protect patients and operators. This guide covers the design of medical power systems using Recom medical-grade converters.

## Medical Safety Standards

### IEC 60601-1 Overview
The international standard for medical electrical equipment requires:
- Two Means of Patient Protection (2xMOPP)
- Limited leakage current
- High isolation voltage
- Fail-safe operation

### Protection Categories

1. **B (Body) Type**: Patient connection with no direct cardiac contact
2. **BF (Body Floating) Type**: Patient connection with floating circuit
3. **CF (Cardiac Floating) Type**: Direct cardiac connection - most stringent

## Isolation Requirements

### 2xMOPP Isolation
Two independent protection barriers between mains and patient:
- Each barrier rated for working voltage
- Redundant protection if one fails
- Reinforced insulation or double insulation

### Isolation Voltage Ratings

| Application | Minimum Isolation |
|-------------|------------------|
| MOOP | 1500VAC |
| 1xMOPP | 3000VAC |
| 2xMOPP | 4000VAC |

## Leakage Current

### Leakage Current Limits

Maximum leakage current for different applications:
- **Earth Leakage**: 500μA (normal), 1000μA (single fault)
- **Enclosure Leakage**: 100μA (normal), 500μA (single fault)
- **Patient Leakage (CF)**: 10μA (normal), 50μA (single fault)

### Leakage Current Budgeting

Calculate total leakage:
1. AC-DC supply leakage: 50-100μA
2. Each DC-DC converter: 10-50μA
3. Filter capacitors: Calculate based on value
4. PCB and cable capacitance: 5-20μA

Design with 20% margin below limits.

## Medical Power Architecture

### Typical Architecture

```
Mains → AC-DC (2xMOPP) → 12V Bus → DC-DC (2xMOPP) → Patient Circuit
```

Key points:
- AC-DC provides first isolation barrier
- DC-DC provides second isolation barrier
- Each barrier rated for 2xMOPP
- Total leakage sum of all components

### Component Selection

1. **AC-DC Supply**:
   - Medical grade with 2xMOPP
   - Low leakage current
   - 4000VAC isolation

2. **DC-DC Converters**:
   - Medical grade (REM series)
   - 2xMOPP rated
   - <50μA leakage each

## Design Checklist

For medical power design:

- [ ] All components medical grade with 2xMOPP
- [ ] Total leakage current budgeted
- [ ] Isolation voltages adequate
- [ ] Creepage and clearance distances met
- [ ] EMC compliance addressed
- [ ] Risk analysis completed
- [ ] Documentation for regulatory submission

## Regulatory Approval

### Testing Requirements

1. **Dielectric Strength**: Hi-pot test at 4000VAC
2. **Leakage Current**: Measure all leakage paths
3. **Ground Impedance**: <0.1Ω for protective earth
4. **Temperature**: Measure under worst-case conditions

### Documentation

Required for regulatory submission:
- Risk management file (ISO 14971)
- Test reports for all safety tests
- Component certifications
- PCB layout drawings
- Schematics with isolation barriers marked

## Conclusion

Medical power design requires careful attention to safety standards. Recom medical-grade converters simplify compliance with pre-certified 2xMOPP isolation. Contact our FAE team for assistance with medical power designs.

faeInsights.insight: Medical power design is all about risk management. Every component choice affects patient safety. The 2xMOPP requirement means you need redundancy in protection. Recom's medical converters provide this redundancy in a single component with reinforced insulation. Leakage current is often the limiting factor - budget carefully and measure early.
faeInsights.logic: Medical design process: First, determine patient contact type (B, BF, or CF). Second, calculate leakage current budget based on limits. Third, select components with appropriate isolation and leakage. Fourth, design PCB with proper creepage and clearance. Fifth, document all isolation barriers for regulatory review.

Leakage current is critical for CF applications
Use only medical-grade components
Document everything for regulatory submission
Test early and often

Use components with medical certifications
Mark all isolation barriers on schematic
Test with worst-case conditions
Start regulatory consultation early
troubleshootingTips: High leakage usually from filter capacitors
Keep detailed test records for submission

faqs.question: What is the difference between MOOP and MOPP?
faqs.answer: MOOP (Means of Operator Protection) protects the operator from electrical hazards. MOPP (Means of Patient Protection) protects the patient and requires higher isolation (4000VAC vs 1500VAC) and lower leakage current. For patient-connected equipment, 2xMOPP is required, meaning two independent protection barriers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for guidance on protection requirements for your medical device.

faqs.question: How do I verify 2xMOPP isolation?
faqs.answer: 2xMOPP isolation is verified through: Dielectric strength testing at 4000VAC; Measurement of creepage and clearance distances; Review of component certifications; Verification of solid insulation thickness. Recom medical converters come with 2xMOPP certification, simplifying your verification process.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for assistance with isolation verification and testing.
